Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only defines variables and a `package()` function that is not executed during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. The global scope contains only standard variable assignments, an `install=` line, and a series of `: "${var}"` no-op expansions which have no side effects. No dangerous commands (eval, curl|bash, base64 decoding, etc.) are present in the global scope or any executed function. The `source` array downloads files from the official Docker GitHub repository (`raw.githubusercontent.com/moby/moby`), which is the expected upstream source. Checksums are provided and pinned to a specific version tag. No obfuscation, unexpected network destinations, or exfiltration patterns are visible. Running the two specified commands is safe.
</details>
<summary>No dangerous code in global scope or source fetching.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dangerous code in global scope or source fetching.[/]

LLM auditresponse for 99-docker-rootless.conf:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a sysctl configuration snippet (placed in `/etc/sysctl.d/`) that sets `kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone=1`. This kernel parameter is required for Docker rootless mode to function properly, as it enables unprivileged user namespace creation. The file contains no executable code, no network requests, no obfuscation, and no references to other files or commands. It is a standard, non-malicious packaging adjustment.
</details>
<summary>Standard sysctl config for Docker rootless; no risk.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD, docker-rootless-extras.install...
+ Reviewed 99-docker-rootless.conf. Status: SAFE -- Standard sysctl config for Docker rootless; no risk.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches scripts from the official moby/moby GitHub repository (raw.githubusercontent.com/moby/moby/...) with pinned version `docker-v${pkgver}` and provides valid SHA-256 checksums. The build process installs these scripts, a systemd socket file, and a sysctl configuration into standard system paths. It uses `awk` and `sed` to extract a systemd service unit from one of the upstream scripts—a transparent and justifiable packaging technique to avoid running an installer that would modify `$HOME`. There are no network requests to unexpected hosts, no obfuscated code, no dangerous commands (curl|bash, eval, base64 decode), and no attempt to exfiltrate or modify data outside the package scope. All operations are standard for an AUR PKGBUILD. The only noteworthy point is the use of `sed` and `awk` on the upstream script, but this is fully explained in comments and serves a legitimate purpose (extracting a service file without executing the script). No security issues.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR PKGBUILD with legitimate upstream sources and safe operations.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing docker-rootless-extras.install...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard AUR PKGBUILD with legitimate upstream sources and safe operations.
LLM auditresponse for docker-rootless-extras.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a standard post-installation script for an AUR package. It runs `sysctl --system` to reload kernel parameters and then prints a help message to the user with instructions on how to configure rootless Docker. There are no network requests, no downloads of code from external sources, no obfuscation, and no dangerous commands like `eval`, `curl`, or `wget`. The behavior is purely informational and follows normal packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>
Informational post-install script, no malicious code.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for dockerd-rootless-29.6.0.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This script is the standard `dockerd-rootless.sh` from Docker's official rootless extras, used to launch dockerd in a rootless environment via rootlesskit. It performs no suspicious activities: it checks for required dependencies (rootlesskit, slirp4netns, etc.), constructs command-line arguments for rootlesskit, and then executes either rootlesskit (in the parent context) or dockerd (in the child context). All network destinations and executables are the package's own upstream tools (rootlesskit, dockerd, slirp4netns, etc.) from standard locations. There is no obfuscated code, no unexpected network requests, no exfiltration of data, and no system modifications outside the package's scope. The use of `exec`, `mount`, `nsenter`, and `sysctl` are normal for this kind of container runtime helper script. The file exactly matches the upstream source for the `docker-rootless-extras` package and contains no injected malicious code.
</details>
<summary>Standard Docker rootless startup script, no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<details>
The script is a legitimate setup tool for Docker rootless mode. It performs standard checks (OS, user, dependencies, subuid/subgid) and installs systemd units or prints manual instructions. All operations are typical for this type of tool: validating prerequisites, creating service files, creating Docker CLI contexts, and providing setup guidance. There is no obfuscation, no unexpected network requests, no code downloading, no exfiltration of data, and no execution of attacker-controlled content. The script only interacts with system configuration in expected ways (e.g., writing systemd unit files under `~/.config/systemd/user/`). No supply-chain attack indicators are present.
</details>
<summary>Standard Docker rootless setup tool, no malicious content.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/docker-rootless-extras.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ab_rejn_...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ab_rejn_
Analyzing docker-rootless-extras AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ab_rejn_) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard package metadata and sources four files: two shell scripts fetched via HTTPS from the official <code>moby/moby</code> GitHub repository under specific tagged versions, and two local configuration files. The <code>source</code> array uses verbatim URLs that point to a trusted upstream (GitHub.com/moby/moby) and are version-pinned via <code>docker-v${pkgver}</code>, reducing the risk of mutable content. The <code>sha256sums</code> are provided for all sources, including the two remote scripts, enabling integrity verification post-download. There is no command substitution, dynamic sourcing, or execution of downloaded content during the sourcing of the PKGBUILD. The <code>pkgver()</code> function is absent, so version computation relies solely on the statically defined <code>pkgver=29.6.0</code>. No top-level logic beyond variable assignments and null commands (<code>:</code>) is present, and these are benign. Thus, running <code>makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare</code> and <code>makepkg --printsrcinfo</code> will safely fetch and verify only the declared sources without executing any risky code.
</details>
<summary>
Pinned, checksummed sources from official upstream; no malicious code executed during sourcing.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Pinned, checksummed sources from official upstream; no malicious code executed during sourcing.[/]

LLM auditresponse for 99-docker-rootless.conf:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `99-docker-rootless.conf` contains a single sysctl configuration line: `kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone=1`. This setting enables unprivileged user namespaces on the system, which is a prerequisite for running Docker in rootless mode. This is consistent with the package’s purpose—supporting rootless Docker operations—and does not introduce malicious behavior.

The change is a standard system configuration adjustment commonly required by container runtimes to allow user-managed namespaces without root privileges. While enabling unprivileged user namespaces can have security implications (e.g., increasing the attack surface for privilege escalation exploits), the setting itself is a documented and expected requirement for rootless containers. It is not inherently malicious, and the file does not contain executable code, network operations, or unauthorized modifications.

This configuration file is installed system-wide via the package manager and would be subject to audit through normal Arch Linux packaging processes. The content is transparent, minimal, and directly related to the software's functionality. There are no obfuscated commands, unexpected downloads, or data exfiltration risks present.
</details>
<summary>
Enables required sysctl for rootless containers; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD, docker-rootless-extras.install...
+ Reviewed 99-docker-rootless.conf. Status: SAFE -- Enables required sysctl for rootless containers; no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for docker-rootless-extras.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `docker-rootless-extras.install` file contains a `post_install()` function that displays a helpful message to the user after package installation. The message explains configuration steps needed to enable Docker's rootless mode on Arch Linux, such as setting up `/etc/subuid` and `/etc/subgid`, enabling the user socket via `systemctl --user`, and exporting the `DOCKER_HOST` environment variable.

The script performs no network operations, file modifications, or system changes beyond printing a static message. Commands shown (e.g., `systemctl --user enable --now docker.socket`) are standard user-level systemd operations and part of Docker’s documented rootless setup. There is no use of `eval`, `base64`, `curl`, or other potentially dangerous constructs, nor any attempt to exfiltrate data or execute remote code.

The content aligns with expected packaging practices for a helper package like `docker-rootless-extras`. It guides users on necessary manual configuration steps, which is common for security-sensitive or complex services like rootless Docker. No obfuscation, unexpected behavior, or supply-chain attack indicators are present.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: Only displays configuration instructions, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed docker-rootless-extras.install. Status: SAFE -- Safe: Only displays configuration instructions, no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches two scripts (`dockerd-rootless.sh` and `dockerd-rootless-setuptool.sh`) from the official `moby/moby` GitHub repository using version-pinned URLs that match the declared `pkgver`. The checksums for all sources are provided and non-skipped, ensuring integrity. The scripts are installed directly or parsed (in the case of the systemd service extraction via `awk` and `sed`) to extract the `docker.service` unit file. No code is downloaded and executed at runtime, and no obfuscated or suspicious commands are present.

The use of `awk` and `sed` to extract and modify the systemd service unit from the setup script is a known workaround to avoid running the full setup tool, which would modify user environments—this is explicitly avoided for safety. The modifications are limited to path substitutions and cleaning up template variables. All installed files are placed in appropriate package locations under `$pkgdir`.

Dependencies and optional dependencies are clearly declared and reasonable for the package's purpose. The `docker-rootless-extras` package supports running Docker without root privileges, and the configuration files installed (`docker.socket`, `99-docker-rootless.conf`) are consistent with that goal. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, unauthorized downloads, or malicious tampering.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: Standard, transparent package for Docker rootless support.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for docker.socket:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file `docker.socket` is a systemd socket unit configuration file that defines a socket for Docker's API. It sets up a Unix socket at `%t/docker.sock` (typically `/run/user/<uid>/docker.sock`) with permissions `0660`, ensuring read-write access is restricted to the owning user and group. The configuration is minimal and adheres to standard systemd socket unit practices.

There are no signs of malicious activity. The file does not execute arbitrary commands, perform network requests, or modify system files outside its scope. It is consistent with normal Docker rootless setup practices, where user-level socket activation is used to bind the Docker daemon securely. The use of `%t` (runtime directory) and restrictive socket permissions align with security best practices for user services.

This file serves a legitimate and expected role in enabling Docker API access in a rootless environment without requiring elevated privileges. No obfuscated code, unexpected downloads, or dangerous operations are present.
</details>
<summary>
Legitimate systemd socket configuration for Docker API</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/3] Reviewing dockerd-rootless-setuptool-29.6.0.sh, dockerd-rootless-29.6.0.sh...
+ Reviewed docker.socket. Status: SAFE -- Legitimate systemd socket configuration for Docker API
LLM auditresponse for dockerd-rootless-setuptool-29.6.0.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided script `dockerd-rootless-setuptool-29.6.0.sh` is a legitimate setup tool for configuring rootless Docker mode. It performs system checks, validates prerequisites (such as user namespace support, `newuidmap`, `iptables`, sysctl settings), and configures systemd user services or provides manual start instructions if systemd is unavailable.

It writes configuration files to standard user-writable locations (`~/.config/systemd/user/`, `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR`, etc.) and installs or uninstalls a user-level systemd service for `dockerd-rootless.sh`. It also manages Docker CLI contexts. All operations are transparent, well-documented, and limited to the user's scope. While it suggests commands that require `sudo` (like installing packages or writing to `/etc/sysctl.d`), it does not execute them directly without user intervention. These are displayed as instructions, requiring explicit admin action.

No obfuscated code, unexpected network calls, or data exfiltration attempts are present. The script fetches no external content at runtime and relies solely on local system inspection and configuration. It aligns with standard Docker documentation for rootless mode and poses no supply-chain risk.
</details>
<summary>Legitimate rootless Docker setup tool, no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing dockerd-rootless-29.6.0.sh...
+ Reviewed dockerd-rootless-setuptool-29.6.0.sh. Status: SAFE -- Legitimate rootless Docker setup tool, no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for dockerd-rootless-29.6.0.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `dockerd-rootless-29.6.0.sh` is a wrapper designed to run `dockerd` in rootless mode using RootlessKit. It performs environment validation (e.g., checking for `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR`, non-root execution), configures networking and mount options based on environment variables, and launches `dockerd` inside a user namespace via `rootlesskit`. All logic is transparent, well-documented, and consistent with the established functionality of Docker's rootless mode.

The script sources environment variables with clear defaults, checks for required binaries (`newuidmap`, `newgidmap`, `rootlesskit`, etc.), and uses safe constructs like `command -v` for discovery. It conditionally enables features (networking, seccomp, mount propagation) based on available tools and configuration. It does not download or execute remote code, exfiltrate data, or modify system files outside of its runtime directory (e.g., under `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/dockerd-rootless`). The use of `exec rootlesskit` to re-execute itself in a namespace is standard behavior for rootless container tooling.

No obfuscation, suspicious payloads, or unauthorized network accesses are present. All operations are locally scoped and aligned with the expected behavior of a Docker rootless mode launcher. The script interacts safely with namespaces, sysctls, and mount points under user control.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code; standard rootless Docker operation
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[3/3] Reviewing ...
